title: Inoculating a Liquid Bacterial Culture
author: Saul Moore
license_note: Confirmed via CrossRef DOI metadata: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.8rxhv7n

steps.title: Obtain LB Broth from the Media kitchen LB Broth contents
steps.instruction: Obtain LB Broth from the Media kitchen LB Broth contents: 4 g NaCl 4 g Tryptone 2 g Yeast Extract dH 2 O to 400 mL

steps.title: Add liquid LB to a tube...
steps.instruction: Add liquid LB to a tube or flask and add the appropriate antibiotic (if required) to the correct concentration (see table below). Antibiotic Concentrations %s %s Note: If you intend to do a mini-prep you will usually want to start 2 mL in a falcon tube, but for larger preps you might want to use as much as a litre of LB in a 2 L Erlenmeyer flask.

steps.title: Using a sterile inoculation loop, select...
steps.instruction: Using a sterile inoculation loop, select a single colony from your bacteria streaked LB agar plate.

steps.title: Dip the inoculation loop into the liquid LB and swirl
steps.instruction: Dip the inoculation loop into the liquid LB and swirl. Discard the inoculation loop.

steps.title: Loosely cover the culture with sterile...
steps.instruction: Loosely cover the culture with sterile aluminium foil or a cap that is not air tight as bacteria needs air.

steps.title: Incubate the bacterial culture at the...
steps.instruction: Incubate the bacterial culture at the required growth temperature overnight (i.e. 12-18 hrs in general) in a shaking incubator.

steps.title: After incubation, check for growth, which...
steps.instruction: After incubation, check for growth, which is characterized by a cloudy haze in the media. Measure the optical density of the bacterial culture at 600nm wavelength using a spectrophotometer. Record the OD600 three times and calculate average, use LB Broth as Blank.
